paragraphs.body: About three weeks ago, we were celebrating a huge win that crowned us ACC regular-season champions in the locker room. It was an unforgettable moment of joy and camaraderie, but after the celebrations were done, I tried to clean up all my stuff, when I noticed my shin guards were missing. At first, it seemed weird to worry over a pair of plastic shields, but it did make me nervous. I ended up finding them a couple of days later and I started to think about why they meant more to me than just a regular pair of plastic shinguards. Those shin guards symbolized my journey, all the games and practices I have played over all those years of playing soccer. It was also a little bit of a superstition because when you have played such a long time with something, you don’t want to switch that. I am also a very superstitious person so that’s probably also why they meant so much to me. They represented a deeply personal connection to my sport.

annotations.text: About three weeks ago, we were celebrating a huge win that crowned us ACC regular-season champions in the locker room.

annotations.comment: You're introducing a lot of ideas in this essay, but I wonder if you could represent these ideas in a more intentional intro form (perhaps a narrative?) rather than just telling or explaining them?
annotations.context_left: why they meant more to me than just a regular pair of plastic shinguards. Those shin guards

paragraphs.body: My Shinguards have been with me throughout almost my whole career as a soccer player. It is very unusual that you use a thing like shinguards for such a long time but there has never been a reason for me to change them and it also has gotten a little bit of a superstition. They are black with a huge neon yellow Nike sign on them. The print has some holes in it, because of all the hits the shinguards have gotten over the years. On the inside, there is like a waffle structure which makes them more flexible. The material is also a little bit softer on the inside so it is more comfortable. You always have to fix them with something like tape or an extra sock so they stay in place. After using them sometimes they start to smell bad so you should rinse them a little bit every week. Even though they aren’t really bothering while playing it is still a feeling of release when you take them off after practice and it makes this clacking sound once they fall to the ground.

annotations.text: They are black with a huge neon yellow Nike sign on them.
annotations.comment: What if you started this description by just describing? In other words, just start here?

paragraphs.body: These shinguards have protected me from a lot of hits throughout all the years of playing soccer. That means they have been with me through a lot of good and bad times. And they have also been along the journey toward my “dream”.

When I was watching all these soccer games my dad would watch, I thought to myself I want to be there at some point. I know many kids have the same dream I have. When I got to the academy and saw people moving out of their country to play for my team I started to think: „These guys are delusional.“ I come from a family, where we think realistic. I have never heard my dad ever saying, that his kid is going pro or anything like that, meanwhile parents with kids that were so much worse than I was talked about their kid going pro. These kids are what make this whole academy thing work and what makes it so difficult to go pro. Because the academies are selling a dream. In my team, we used to earn little to no money, and people would move across the country or even from other countries and that would obviously cost them money, but they were willing to take that risk and pay that money just to have a shot at going pro. Maybe 1 guy out of each class gets a pro contract, and every year people get kicked out of the academy. The chance to make it pro is like 3% even when you are in an academy. Dreams are what make people stop thinking about chances or probabilities. In my opinion, it is a beautiful thing to have dreams and chase them, but I have also seen a lot of people's dreams getting destroyed throughout the years, that is why I am always thinking more realistic than optimistic. Dreams are something that inspires people, something that motivates people, something that makes people work harder.

annotations.text: Dreams are something that inspires people, something that motivates people, something that makes people work harder.
annotations.comment: interesting idea. I wonder if you could develop them in a more extended definition paragraph? Let's talk about the potential power of the definition paragraph to point your reader to your essay's central focusing idea.

annotations.context_right: never heard my dad ever saying, that his kid is going pro or anything like that, meanwhile

paragraphs.body: These shinguards are also something that protected my dream. Because as a soccer player, you learn at a very young age: “Your body is your capital.” That basically means that you are going to need your body to succeed later in your career. If you get hurt your career might be over. And with that, your dream is also over. That is another big part of having dreams. You sometimes can’t even influence if they will become true or not. That’s also what makes it a dream and not a goal. I know a lot of guys who made all the investments and all the right choices throughout their youth, but then they got hurt and their career was over. Whether you get hurt or not is oftentimes not in your hands, someone could just tackle you and break your leg, and with that, your career would never be the same.

annotations.text: Whether you get hurt or not is oftentimes not in your hands, someone could just tackle you and break your leg, and with that, your career would never be the same.
annotations.comment: It's interesting that these ideas are related to your realistic views of your future in soccer. I'm curious about this connection and would love to talk about it further. I think the friction you're introducing, here, might lead us to a clear central focusing idea.

paragraphs.body: The word swefn, which meant „sleeping vision“ in Middle English was replaced by the word dream. The meaning of the word dream I want to talk about, which means something like an ideal came with the term „dream girl“, which was the term to describe a girl that was perfect and almost impossible to get. That’s how the word dream became the term for an ideal thing that is unlikely to happen. This last sentence is a really good definition of a dream. But for me personally, it is a goal that drives you to be something greater than you can even think of being. That’s what having a dream is all about. That feeling you have when chasing something that isn’t even close to reach.

annotations.text: But for me personally, it is a goal that drives you to be something greater than you can even think of being. That’s what having a dream is all about. That feeling you have when chasing something that isn’t even close to reach.
annotations.comment: I'm interested. This is a repetition of idea, so it must be important! Let's talk :)

paragraphs.body: A dream is not just a series of events that happen in your mind when you sleep. It is a goal that inspires you and motivates you. A dream is not a rigid plan or checklist, nor something entirely within your control. It’s not limited to sleep or fleeting thoughts. A dream isn’t a guarantee of happiness, and it isn’t static or unchanging. A dream isn’t perfect, and it does not always bring comfort—it can challenge or even frustrate. A dream is a spark, a small percentage of possibility born from your imagination and desires. It’s both personal and universal, connecting us through hope and ambition. A dream fuels action, shaping your choices and inspiring you to grow. It’s fragile yet resilient, a canvas where reality meets possibility. It is something that drives you, something that inspires you to work hard even though you don’t even know if it will pay off. Above all, to dream is to believe in something greater than yourself. The dream is also what drove me to get better every day and what made me go through what I describe in my narrative paragraph. It's also what drove all the other people I tell you about in my soccer story and what made them want to become great at what they were doing.

annotations.text: dream fuels action, shaping your choices and inspiring you to grow.
annotations.comment: Yes. I think this idea is also important to your essay's central focusing idea. I wonder: what happens if you don't reach your dreams? Was it all for waste? Or are there other benefits? Other "wins"?
